raw_case:
Differentiating appendiceal neoplasm from perforated appendiceal diverticulum in chronic appendicitis: a case report


Abstract

Low mucinous neoplasm of the appendix (LAMN) and appendiceal diverticulum are both uncommon pathologies that may pose a diagnostic challenge. Both may present as either appendicitis or asymptomatically and have a risk of perforation. LAMN, carries the additional risk of pseudomyxoma pertitonei and metastasis. Ensuring correct histopathology is crucial, as computed tomography (CT) abdomen/pelvis may only demonstrate a mildly dilated appendix, delaying diagnostic laparoscopy and appendicectomy. Here, we describe the case report of a 56-year-old woman who presented with chronic intermittent right iliac fossa pain initially determined to be chronic appendicitis. Following laparoscopic appendicectomy, histopathology demonstrated LAMN, however, on further re- assessment of histopathology, as well as the completion of a normal pan-CT and colonoscopy, a final diagnosis of ruptured appendiceal diverticulum was made. Our case demonstrates the utility of a multi-disciplinary approach in evaluating patients with possible appendiceal LAMN or appendiceal diverticulum.

Introduction

Diverticulum of the appendix and low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (LAMN) are rare pathologies. The incidence of appendiceal diverticulum ranges from 0.004% and 2.1% globally based on appendicectomy discovery [1, 2]. Appendiceal diverticulum are most common in adults over 30 and more predominant in males [3]. LAMN are equally rare; in a large population study conducted in Germany, the incidence of LAMN of 108 247 appendectomies was 0.13% [4]. LAMN occurred predominantly in women with an average age of 61 on discovery [4]. Due to the growth pattern of the LAMN, LAMN can initially appear similar to appendiceal diverticulum [5]. Here, we present a case in which a patient with chronic right iliac fossa (RIF) pain is found to have an appendiceal diverticulum initially diagnosed as LAMN.

Case presentation

A 56-year-old female presented to her general practitioner with chronic intermittent RIF for months. She was otherwise well with no systemic symptoms. Regular medications included meloxicam 15 mg daily. Previous surgical history was significant for left oophorectomy for a dermoid cyst and total hysterectomy. They had no previous colonoscopies or gastroscopies and no family history of colorectal cancer. On initial examination, she appeared well with vital signs within normal range and exhibited RIF tenderness to deep palpation. Laboratory tests demonstrated normal white cells and liver function test. A pelvic ultrasound demonstrated a mildly prominent appendix 10 mm in diameter close to the anterior wall where the patient was tender. The distal tip of the appendix demonstrated a small echogenic region of 8x5mm which was thought to demonstrate collapse or debris. There was adjacent echogenic fat and three hypoechoic rounded lymph nodes, the largest measuring 12 mm. An outpatient computer tomography (CT) abdominal/pelvis with intravenous contrast was performed 4 days following this, demonstrating a mildly thickened appendix with a maximum diameter of 11 mm with mild mucosal enhancement, no fat stranding and a small appendiceal diverticula. The peri appendiceal fat plane also appeared normal, as demonstrated in Fig. 1.Owing to the persistence of RIF pain and CT evidence of appendicitis, the provisional diagnosis of chronic appendicitis was made, and the patient was referred for laparoscopic appendicectomy. An appendicetomy was performed 5 days following the initial referral. Intra-operatively, the appendix was injected and thickened with an oedematous mesoappendix. The patient recovered well and was discharged following the operation. Histopathology demonstrated the appendix measured 70 mm x 12 mm x 12 mm with the attached mesoappendix measuring 70 mm x 25 mm. The lumen of the appendix was filled with gelatinous material. A neoplastic lesion was noted in the proximal 2/3 of appendix, measuring 45 mm, and was classified as a LAMN, with American Joint Committee on Cancer staging of pT3N0MX. One mesoappendiceal lymph node demonstrated no evidence of malignancy. A two-month follow-up colonoscopy was unremarkable and demonstrated no polyps, as revealed in Fig. 2. Follow-up CT chest and abdomen/pelvis demonstrated no evidence of metastases. Tumour markers were normal, with carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) <1.7μg/L, CA-199 10U/ml and CA-125 22U/ml.Due to the finding of LAMN, opinions were sought through multidisciplinary team meetings (MDTs) with medical and surgical oncologists. Additionally, opinions from peritonectomy teams were obtained. The MDT concluded that based on the LAMN confinement to the appendix, the risk of future pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP) was low, and the patient did not require further surgical intervention. An additional opinion regarding histology was obtained, which determined the specimen was in most keeping with an appendiceal diverticulum rather than a LAMN, supporting the decision that no further treatment or surgical resection was required.

Discussion

Both appendiceal diverticulum and LAMN are rare pathologies often found incidentally following appendectomy [1, 2, 4]. Although the incidence of LAMN is rare, a prompt diagnosis is essential due to the potential for developing PMP. The 5-year survival rates of LAMN range from 91% in localized disease to 23% in cases of poorly differentiated proliferation [6–8]. In rare cases, LAMN has been associated with extra-abdominal metastases, particularly pulmonary [6]. Although a non-neoplastic presentation, appendiceal diverticulum also presents a risk for delayed diagnosis due to the risk of perforation. An appendiceal diverticulum can be congenital and acquired both with a 3-fold increase of perforation compared with perforation with a structurally normal appendix in cases of acute appendicitis [9]. Although both pathologies carry risks with delayed detection and treatment, they can be asymptomatic or present as chronic or acute appendicitis.Chronic appendicitis is a relatively uncommon cause of recurrent abdominal pain, only reported in 1.5% of appendicitis cases [10]. LAMN are often asymptomatic and are an incidental finding in the context of acute appendicitis or following the development of PMP post appendiceal rupture and peritoneal spread. Appendiceal diverticulum is also attributed to acute appendicitis presentations, with few cases reporting it in conjunction with chronic appendicitis [1]. The individual rarity of the pathologies presented in this case posed a diagnostic challenge. Due to the unspecific histological features of LAMN with proliferation restricted to muscularis propria, it can appear similar to a diverticulum, leading to difficulties in differentiating LAMN from an appendiceal diverticulum [11, 12].Differentiating LAMN from appendiceal diverticulum can be challenging, as seen in this case. Although initial histology described a LAMN lesion, the second opinion described it as likely appendiceal diverticulum aligned with the benign colonoscopy and normal tumour markers. It is important to note that in some cases, LAMN has been found in conjunction with appendiceal diverticulum, further adding to the complexity of pathological differentiation. In a retrospective review by Chan et al. diverticulum of the appendix was found to be associated with an appendiceal neoplasm in 17.5% of cases [13], therefore demonstrating the continuing challenge of differentiation of LAMN from appendiceal diverticulum. The management of these pathologies differs: the appendiceal diverticulum does not require further management following laparoscopic appendicectomy. LAMN requires surgical resection of ileocecal lymph nodes and subsequent peritonectomy with or without hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy, depending on the intra- abdominal metastases. In the setting of complex pathological differentiation, a multidisciplinary approach to management is crucial to diagnosis and definitive management, as apparent in this case.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 
  - 56-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** 
  - Chronic intermittent right iliac fossa (RIF) pain for several months.
- **History of Present Illness:** 
  - Persistent RIF tenderness noted over months, with imaging findings suggestive of appendicitis and appendiceal diverticulum.
- **Past Medical History:** 
  - Left oophorectomy for dermoid cyst and total hysterectomy.
- **Personal History:** 
  - Regular medication includes meloxicam (15 mg daily).
- **Family History:** 
  - No family history of colorectal cancer.
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - RIF tenderness on deep palpation; vital signs unremarkable.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - **Ultrasound:** Mildly prominent appendix (10 mm diameter) with adjacent lymph nodes and echogenic fat; suspected diverticulum.  
  - **CT Scan (abdomen/pelvis):** Appendix mildly thickened (11 mm diameter), presence of appendiceal diverticulum, no fat stranding, or peri-appendiceal abnormalities.  
  - **Laparoscopic appendicectomy:** Thickened appendix exhibiting gelatinous material; histopathology initially reported low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN, AJCC stage pT3N0MX).  
  - **Follow-up Tests:** Normal colonoscopy, normal CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with no signs of metastasis, normal tumor markers including CEA, CA-19.9, and CA-125.  
  - **Histopathology Review:** Revised diagnosis favored ruptured appendiceal diverticulum over LAMN.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnoses:**
   - Chronic appendicitis.
   - Appendiceal neoplasm (LAMN).
   - Appendiceal diverticulum.

2. **Step-by-Step Analysis:**
   - **Chronic Appendicitis:** Considered due to persistent RIF pain and imaging findings of thickened appendix; ultimately ruled out since histology revealed pathology not consistent with chronic inflammation alone.  
   - **LAMN:** Initially favored due to findings of gelatinous material in the appendiceal lumen, abnormal architecture on histology, and AJCC staging based on first pathology report. However, lack of metastatic spread on imaging, normal tumor markers, and revised histology suggesting absence of significant neoplastic features led to exclusion.  
   - **Appendiceal Diverticulum:** Suggested by findings on imaging (appendiceal diverticulum) and final histopathology demonstrating features consistent with diverticulum rather than neoplasia. 

3. **Conclusion:** A second opinion on histopathology and benign follow-up findings clarified that the presentation was most consistent with a ruptured appendiceal diverticulum, not LAMN. Lack of systemic or metastatic involvement further supported this conclusion.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Ruptured appendiceal diverticulum with no evidence of low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN) or malignancy.

---

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and Initial Presentation:** The patient is a 56-year-old female presenting with chronic intermittent right iliac fossa (RIF) pain for several months. This is the primary symptom driving the investigation.

<step 2> **Evaluate Imaging Findings:** Ultrasound and CT scans both indicated a mildly prominent/thickened appendix and the presence of an appendiceal diverticulum. These findings initially suggested appendicitis and were consistent with the location of the patient's pain.

<step 3> **Consider Surgical Intervention and Initial Histopathology:** A laparoscopic appendicectomy was performed, and the appendix was noted to contain gelatinous material. The initial histopathology report indicated a low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN), AJCC stage pT3N0MX. This was a concerning diagnosis suggesting a potentially malignant condition.

<step 4> **Assess Follow-up Investigations:**  Normal colonoscopy, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (no metastasis), and tumor markers (CEA, CA-19.9, CA-125) were all performed and found to be normal. These results were somewhat discordant with a pT3 LAMN, which would typically warrant closer monitoring or further intervention and might be associated with elevated tumor markers in some cases or signs of spread in imaging.

<step 5> **Focus on the Revised Histopathology:** The crucial piece of information is the revised histopathology report, which favored "ruptured appendiceal diverticulum over LAMN." This revision is significant and changes the entire diagnostic picture.  A ruptured appendiceal diverticulum can explain the chronic RIF pain, the imaging findings, and even the gelatinous material (which could be mucin from the diverticulum).

<step 6> **Integrate all Findings to reach the Final Diagnosis:** The revised histopathology is the most definitive diagnostic test in this case.  The normal follow-up tests further support the benign nature of the condition and are consistent with the revised diagnosis of a ruptured appendiceal diverticulum. The initial concern of LAMN is effectively ruled out by the expert pathology review.

<step 7> **Formulate the Final Diagnosis:** Based on the revised histopathology report and the overall clinical picture, the final diagnosis is **ruptured appendiceal diverticulum**. This diagnosis is consistent with all the available clinical and pathological information.

### Answer:
Ruptured appendiceal diverticulum

reasoning_eval.step: **Analyze the Chief Complaint and Initial Presentation:** The patient is a 56-year-old female presenting with chronic intermittent right iliac fossa (RIF) pain for several months. This is the primary symptom driving the investigation.

**Evaluate Imaging Findings:** Ultrasound and CT scans both indicated a mildly prominent/thickened appendix and the presence of an appendiceal diverticulum. These findings initially suggested appendicitis and were consistent with the location of the patient's pain.

**Consider Surgical Intervention and Initial Histopathology:** A laparoscopic appendicectomy was performed, and the appendix was noted to contain gelatinous material. The initial histopathology report indicated a low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN), AJCC stage pT3N0MX. This was a concerning diagnosis suggesting a potentially malignant condition.

**Assess Follow-up Investigations:**  Normal colonoscopy, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (no metastasis), and tumor markers (CEA, CA-19.9, CA-125) were all performed and found to be normal. These results were somewhat discordant with a pT3 LAMN, which would typically warrant closer monitoring or further intervention and might be associated with elevated tumor markers in some cases or signs of spread in imaging.

**Focus on the Revised Histopathology:** The crucial piece of information is the revised histopathology report, which favored "ruptured appendiceal diverticulum over LAMN." This revision is significant and changes the entire diagnostic picture.  A ruptured appendiceal diverticulum can explain the chronic RIF pain, the imaging findings, and even the gelatinous material (which could be mucin from the diverticulum).

**Integrate all Findings to reach the Final Diagnosis:** The revised histopathology is the most definitive diagnostic test in this case.  The normal follow-up tests further support the benign nature of the condition and are consistent with the revised diagnosis of a ruptured appendiceal diverticulum. The initial concern of LAMN is effectively ruled out by the expert pathology review.

**Formulate the Final Diagnosis:** Based on the revised histopathology report and the overall clinical picture, the final diagnosis is **ruptured appendiceal diverticulum**. This diagnosis is consistent with all the available clinical and pathological information.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Initial differential diagnoses include chronic appendicitis, appendiceal neoplasm (LAMN), and appendiceal diverticulum.

<Step 2> Chronic appendicitis considered due to persistent right lower quadrant (RIF) pain and imaging findings of a thickened appendix; ruled out as histology showed pathology inconsistent with chronic inflammation.

<Step 3> Appendiceal neoplasm (LAMN) initially favored by findings of gelatinous material in the appendiceal lumen, abnormal architectural features on histology, and AJCC staging from the first pathology report; excluded due to lack of metastatic spread on imaging, normal tumor markers, and revised histology indicating absence of significant neoplastic features.
<Step 4> Appendiceal diverticulum suggested by imaging findings of an appendiceal diverticulum and final histopathology showing features consistent with diverticulum rather than neoplasia.
<Step 5> Second opinion on histopathology and benign follow-up findings confirmed the presentation was most consistent with a ruptured appendiceal diverticulum, not LAMN.

<Step 6> Lack of systemic or metastatic involvement further supports the diagnosis of a ruptured appendiceal diverticulum without evidence of low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN) or malignancy.
Final diagnosis: Ruptured appendiceal diverticulum with no evidence of low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (LAMN) or malignancy.
